title: Signatures of the Quantum Geometric Dipole of Interlayer Excitons in Counterflow Conductivity
authors: Fanuel I. Mendez, Luis Brey, H. A. Fertig
abstract: Collective excitations of many-body electron systems can carry internal structure, supporting novel quantum geometric and topological properties. Among these are a quantum geometric dipole (QGD), which for excitons have direct significance as an internal polarization. For interlayer excitons of a bilayer system, this represents an in-plane dipole moment, which can be used to drive them with in-plane electric fields. In this work, we consider counterflow electric currents associated with driven excitons in such a bilayer system as a probe of their QGD structure. As a simple but non-trivial example, we analyze a structure with a one-dimensional periodic potential in a strong perpendicular magnetic field. The resulting magnetoexciton bands host QGD structure that distinguishes it from the exciton QGD of a uniform system. To model exciton transport we adopt a Boltzmann approach that includes inter-band tunneling, allowing us to consider non-equilibrium momentum distributions that result from strong layer-antisymmetric driving fields. We show how linear response to a layer-symmetric component of the driving fields provide information about the QGD, and that the broad QGD structure of the exciton bands can be probed by the varying the layer-antisymmetric field. Our results demonstrate that counterflow conductivity serves as a tunable probe of the internal quantum geometric structure carried by the interlayer excitons, connecting transport to the quantum geometry of many-body excitations.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the counterflow conductivity of interlayer excitons can serve as a tunable probe for their internal quantum geometric dipole. By manipulating periodic potentials and electric fields, one can observe how the quantum geometry of many-body excitations influences macroscopic transport.
main_problem: The paper investigates how the internal quantum geometric dipole (QGD) of interlayer excitons manifests in transport properties, specifically looking for experimental signatures in counterflow conductivity.
main_result: The authors show that the off-diagonal counterflow conductivity $\sigma_{xy}^{(CF)}$ is proportional to the gradient of the QGD with respect to momentum, and that this conductivity can be tuned by varying the periodic potential strength and the layer-antisymmetric electric field.
method: The study employs a semiclassical Boltzmann transport approach including inter-band Landau-Zener tunneling and numerical diagonalization of a tridiagonal Hamiltonian in an extended zone scheme.
model_or_system: A 2D bilayer system (such as TMDs or graphene heterostructures) in a strong perpendicular magnetic field, subject to a unidirectional 1D periodic potential (superlattice).
key_observables: Counterflow conductivity tensor components ($\sigma_{xy}^{(CF)}$, $\sigma_{yx}^{(CF)}$, $\sigma_{xx}^{(CF)}$, $\sigma_{yy}^{(CF)}$) and the Quantum Geometric Dipole (QGD).
important_parameters: Periodic potential strength ($W$), layer-antisymmetric electric field ($E_-$), dielectric constant ($\kappa$), magnetic length ($\ell$), and layer separation ($d$).
main_assumptions: Strong magnetic field limit (lowest Landau level), high-temperature/dilute limit (ignoring BEC and exciton-exciton interactions), and neglect of electron/hole recombination.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows schematics of exciton counterflow and the QGD definition; Figure 2 illustrates the QGD vector structure and its dependence on potential strength; Figure 3 shows interband tunneling at avoided crossings; Figure 4 displays exciton band structures; Figure 6 shows conductivity dependence on $\kappa$ and $E_-$.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the QGD and its relation to exciton polarization, moves to the semiclassical equations of motion and the Boltzmann transport framework, analyzes the effects of periodic potentials and electric fields on conductivity, and concludes with an appendix deriving the explicit QGD-conductivity link.
why_interesting: It provides a theoretical bridge between the abstract quantum geometry of many-body wavefunctions and measurable transport coefficients, which is highly relevant for understanding topological transport in correlated bilayer systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Schematic of interlayer exciton counterflow current in a bilayer system. Electric fields applied individually to the electron and hole layers give rise to symmetric (E+) and antisymmetric (E−) fields. In semiclassical equations of motion, the symmetric field couples to the quantum geometric dipole and drives the center of mass velocity ˙R+ whereas E−induces time evolution of the exciton center-of-mass momentum, impacting the exciton dipole moment. (b) Schematic of the dipole moment in a bilayer system. The quantum geometric dipole moment is a gauge-invariant quantity given by the difference of the Berry connections of the hole and electron, constructed from cell-periodic...

Figure 2. Quantum geometric dipole Dn(K) of a magnetoexciton in a strong magnetic field in a periodic potential. (a) Vector plot of the QGD in a bilayer system in a unidirectional periodic potential for the lowest band (n = 0). (b) Band-projected QGD for fixed Ky = 0 for varying periodic potential strengths W (meV) in the extended zone scheme. In (b), the dashed vertical lines indicate avoided crossings where the QGD vanishes. Inset: Behavior of the QGD for large momentum magnitude in extended zone scheme. Parameters for (a): ℓ= 100 ˚A, d = 3 nm, a = 140 nm , κ = 3.76, W = 0.1 meV and Nc = 2. Parameters for (b): ℓ= 100 ˚A, d = 3 nm, a = 140 nm , κ = 3.76 and Nc = 20.

Figure 3. Schematic an avoided crossing between two exciton bands at Kx = 0 with gap ∆. Arrows along each band indi- cate the direction of the exciton flow driven by E−. Dashed ar- rows crossing the gap represent the interband tunneling rates Γm→n and Γn→m. The distribution is divided into regions f (−)(Kx) and f (+)(Kx) corresponding to Kx < 0 and Kx > 0, respectively.

Figure 5. Boltzmann distribution function in the extended zone scheme, for (a) E−= 0.1 kV/cm while varying different values of the periodic potential strength W, and (b) W = 0.09 meV for different values of the antisymmetric electric field E−. Other parameters are T = 10 K, ℓ= 100 ˚A, d = 3 nm, κ = 3.76, a = 140 nm, τ = 1 ps, N = 19 and nexc = 109 cm−2. Note discontinuities at avoided crossings, due to interband tunneling.

Figure 4. Band structure En(Kx) for Ky = 0 for the low- est five bands of the magnetoexciton for (a) small periodic potential strength W = 0.09 meV and (b) large periodic po- tential strength W = 0.5 meV. The dashed vertical lines cor- respond to the avoided crossings at Kx = 0 and Kx = ±π/a. As W increases, the gaps open significantly and the lowest band becomes dispersionless. The parameters are: ℓ= 100˚A, d = 3 nm, a = 140 nm, κ = 3.76 and N = 19.
